danger_signals.visual_form: Hooded soldiers (Maskers) in varied colored outfits; ninjas; mutated creatures (Gelzos, Blogas, lava men)
danger_signals.meaning: Active threat requiring evasion or combat; color variance indicates different attack patterns and threat levels
Instant death hazard; player must take cover or avoid entirely

danger_signals.visual_form: Falling without floor/platform; time expiration countdown
danger_signals.meaning: Instant death conditions requiring platforming precision

danger_signals.visual_form: Weapon downgrade from submachine gun to pistol to single slow 'chaser' bullet
danger_signals.meaning: Escalating vulnerability state; visual degradation of player offensive capability

danger_signals.visual_form: Life meter drains by half per physical hit; two-hit death limit
danger_signals.meaning: Proximity danger indicator; visible remaining health creates tension

safety_signals.visual_form: Marked doors on stages labeled 'bullets' or 'arms'
safety_signals.meaning: Safe cover positions and ammunition resupply locations; hiding/tactical retreat points
Cover objects for taking defensive positions against enemy fire
Safe traversal paths; vertical movement options for evasion

faction_indicators.faction: WCPO (World Crime Police Organization) / Rolling Thunder Unit
faction_indicators.symbol_or_sigil: Rolling Thunder espionage unit insignia (implied through protagonist affiliation)
faction_indicators.environmental_markers: Standard-issue pistol and submachine gun equipment; professional spy aesthetic

faction_indicators.color_identity: Multiple color variants for Maskers indicating organizational hierarchy or specialization
faction_indicators.symbol_or_sigil: Geldra leader Maboo as final boss/symbolic enemy
faction_indicators.enemy_visual_variants: Maskers in hooded outfits with color-coded variants (grenaders vs. kneeling shooters); ninjas; mutated creatures (Gelzos, panthers, Blogas, lava men)
faction_indicators.environmental_markers: Secret society bases in New York; dungeon-like stages; final Maboo fortress

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Five stages with standard enemy placement and trap configurations; in-game monitor displays scenes of Leila's capture and torture
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Standard enemy patterns; predictable stage design

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Five stages visually similar to Story 1 but with increased complexity
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: More traps; different enemy placement; heightened difficulty signaled through gameplay intensity rather than visual environmental change

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Torture scenes displayed on in-game monitor screen at end of each stage
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Reinforces narrative stakes and enemy faction control over hostage; visual reminder of rescue mission objective

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Geldra leader Maboo as distinct enemy entity replacing standard Masker types
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Ultimate combat challenge; rescue objective becomes achievable upon defeat

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Pistol → Submachine gun visual upgrade; downgrade to single 'chaser' bullet when ammo exhausted
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Player capability directly reflects available resources; visual weapon degradation mirrors tactical disadvantage

environmental_storytelling.example: Ten stages split across two stories progressing from Geldra infiltration to final confrontation; stage variety (caves, corridors, fortress) communicates deeper penetration into enemy territory
In-game large monitor displays torture scenes of captured agent Leila at stage conclusions; establishes urgency and emotional stakes for rescue mission

environmental_storytelling.technique: Enemy type variety as faction diversity
environmental_storytelling.example: Geldra employs specialized unit types (grenadiers, kneeling shooters, ninjas, mutated creatures) suggesting diverse military/supernatural organization; visual variety reinforces organizational scope
Crates, obstacles, and doorways communicate tactical stage design; environment suggests hit-and-run espionage gameplay rather than frontal assault
Doors labeled 'bullets' and 'arms' communicate safe points and resupply stations; visual language guides player through hostile territory

entity_behavioral_tells.behavior: Some throw grenades only; others shoot while kneeling; color coding provides visual threat assessment
Slow, continuous fire limited; requires strategic ammo conservation
Rapid continuous fire via button hold; heightened offensive capability visually expressed through weapon upgrade
Single slow 'chaser' bullet per trigger; severe vulnerability state indicated by weapon degradation

entity_behavioral_tells.entity: Various enemies (ninjas, Gelzos, Blogas, panthers, lava men)
entity_behavioral_tells.behavior: Distinct attack patterns and movement behaviors; visual design communicates threat type and required tactical response
Ultimate combat challenge; defeat unlocks mission success and Leila's rescue

notes: Rolling Thunder (NES 1989) relies heavily on visual color-coding and entity design to communicate threat levels and faction membership. The game establishes world-state through two parallel narrative tracks (Story 1 normal / Story 2 hard), differentiated through enemy placement and trap complexity rather than visual environment changes. Danger is primarily communicated through: (1) enemy visual design and color variance indicating attack patterns, (2) visible weapon degradation reflecting resource scarcity, (3) life meter showing damage accumulation, and (4) instant-death hazards (projectiles, falls, timeout). Safety is signaled through doorway locations (marked 'bullets'/'arms'), physical cover objects (crates), and platform navigation paths. The Geldra faction is characterized through diverse enemy types (Maskers with color variants, ninjas, mutated creatures) suggesting organizational sophistication. The hostage rescue narrative is reinforced through in-game monitor displays showing Leila's torture at stage conclusions. The two-story structure (10 stages total) communicates narrative progression without significant environmental visual changes—difficulty escalation is behavioral/mechanical rather than aesthetic.
